name: Platform-Neutral Governance and Certification Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Dependence on Apple/Google's platform signals remains the default path, sustaining single-point-of-failure risk. → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through diversified anchors and standardized APIs enabling 2–4 fallback providers to onboard within 6–12 months. → Strategic: Strengthens Danish digital sovereignty and resilience, reducing supplier concentration and aligning with EU eIDAS2 resilience goals. Trade-off: Gains in sovereignty may slow procurement cycles and increase governance overhead.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral governance framework with explicit procurement-neutral standards, privacy-by-design, and an open certification program spanning NSIS and eIDAS.
Mandate multi-anchor trust through diverse cryptographic standards and a cross-provider audit trail with standardized fallback channels.
Launch a radical self-sovereign identity wallet pilot backed by open-source components and a government-backed revenue-sharing model to sustain an ecosystem.
review: Trade-off: Controls Speed of deployment vs Platform sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the ongoing governance cost and cross-border certification timelines, and the need for sustainable funding.

name: Procurement & Standards Intervention
consequences: Immediate: Procurement contracts begin embedding platform-neutral fallback requirements. → Systemic: 25% faster onboarding of diverse suppliers due to standardized APIs and a common certification baseline. → Strategic: Improves resilience and sovereignty, but Trade-off: higher upfront negotiation costs and potential longer cycle times.
options: Embed mandatory platform-neutral fallback clauses in all future identity procurement contracts and NSIS integration guidelines.
Create a Danish interoperability framework that requires fallback-paths and cross-provider certification as preconditions for any MitID-adjacent extension.
Implement a radical modular procurement model with a government-backed open-source fallback-provider marketplace that funds and certifies independent vendors on a subscription basis.
review: Trade-off: Controls Cost efficiency vs Supplier diversification. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term funding stability beyond initial procurement cycles and possible fragmentation without central oversight.

name: Demonstration Program & Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators validate standard-based fallback feasibility in controlled environments. → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through cross-platform interoperability testing and shared test signals enabling broader uptake. → Strategic: Reduces vendor lock-in and enables EU interoperability; Trade-off: higher development complexity and coordination burden.
options: Develop non-production demonstrators showing standard-based fallback using WebAuthn/OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS.
Extend demonstrators to include browser-based or hardware-token fallback with cross-platform telemetry and a sandboxed identity-provider emulator.
Pilot a cross-border, privacy-preserving fallback network using decentralized identifiers (DID) and verifiable credentials (VC) with a token-based incentive model.
review: Trade-off: Controls Feasibility of demonstration vs Regulatory coordination burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider alignment with EU-wide testbeds and data-sharing constraints.

name: Resilience Financing & Continuity
consequences: Immediate: Additional resilience budget lines approved. → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through shared funding and risk-sharing enabling more robust pilots. → Strategic: Enhances continuity and sovereignty, but Trade-off: sustained funding commitments create governance complexity and potential political exposure.
options: Establish a government-backed resilience fund to underwrite platform-neutral fallback pilots.
Create a risk-sharing framework with industry partners and academic consortia to fund non-production demonstrators and procurement policy advocacy.
Launch a revenue-sharing resilience network that pools private contributions to sustain open fallback identity services, with a subscription model for public-sector usage.
review: Trade-off: Controls Budget consolidation vs Independent resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term sustainability and governance of shared funds, as well as risk of political cycles.

name: EU Alignment & International Collaboration
consequences: Immediate: EU policy engagement initiated with AltID/eIDAS2 bodies. → Systemic: 25% faster cross-border interoperability milestones via shared standards and mutual recognition. → Strategic: Positions Denmark as a platform-neutral leader; Trade-off: potential friction with domestic procurement preferences and sovereignty concerns.
options: Engage EU bodies to incorporate platform-neutral language into AltID and eIDAS2, with formal inputs.
Co-develop a joint EU procurement directive enabling shared standards and mutual recognition for fallback paths.
Catalyze an international alliance for platform-neutral public digital identities, including cross-border pilots using open standards, DID/SSI, and a shared funding consortium.
review: Trade-off: Controls National sovereignty vs EU interoperability. Weakness: The options fail to consider domestic industry impact and regulatory friction with EU standards.

name: Platform-Neutral Governance & Procurement
consequences: Immediate: Early procurement tests reveal reliance on single suppliers → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through modular adapters and shared risk frameworks → Strategic: reduces single-vendor exposure and strengthens resilience, at higher coordination costs.
options: Institute formal platform-neutrality procurement criteria and pilot a lightweight AltID adapter layer that routes authentication through non-Apple/Google channels while preserving MitID trust anchors in a sandbox.

Develop a standardized platform-agnostic trust API suite and a staged procurement framework enabling open-source components, third-party validators, and explicit diversification milestones across critical identity surfaces.
Launch an open EU-aligned platform-agnostic identity broker powered by a decentralized identity network (DID/Verifiable Credentials) with a community-governed funding model.
review: Trade-off: Controls Platform Sovereignty vs. Vendor Lock-in. Weakness: The options fail to consider EU procurement friction and data localization.

name: Resilience-Centric Technical Demonstration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrator results show viable fallback concepts for authentication → Systemic: 25% faster iteration cycles through reusable components and shared testbeds → Strategic: diminishes OS vendor dependence, yet demands deeper inter-organizational alignment and funding discipline.
options: Assemble non-production demos that demonstrate WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on privacy-respecting Android devices, validating interoperability while keeping MitID core trust anchors intact.
Construct two demonstrators: a mobile-oriented fallback and a hardware-token-based fallback path, with standardized interfaces and security reviews gating progression.
Deploy an end-to-end cross-stack demo using on-device attestation, federated identity federation, and edge-secure enclaves to show real-world resilience independent of OS vendors.
review: Trade-off: Controls Complexity vs. Security Certainty. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-vendor integration timelines and regulatory review cycles.

name: Standards Adoption & EU Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Regulatory messaging shifts toward platform neutrality → Systemic: 25% faster adaptation cycles through shared standards and pre-approved templates → Strategic: broadens interoperability, but slows domestic policy pacing.
options: Engage EU and NSIS bodies to align with eIDAS2 standards, publish a lightweight Danish interoperability roadmap, and ensure procurement language supports platform-neutral fallback.
Co-author a Danish AltID interoperability spec with NSIS adapters, W3C verifiable credentials, and OpenID Connect recipe to ensure robust fallback options.
Establish a joint EU-DK standards initiative to codify a mandatory platform-neutral fallback protocol, backed by a regulatory sandbox enabling rapid testing across member states.
review: Trade-off: Controls Regulatory Alignment vs. National Autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider practical regional procurement constraints and budgetary cycles.

name: Economic & Financing Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Budget discipline reduces waste → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through shared platforms and cross-program reuse → Strategic: resilience funding improves autonomy but risks slower stakeholder consensus.
options: Institute staged funding with milestone gates, formal risk registers, and external security reviews, ensuring contingency budgeting without overcommitment.
Form a public-private coalition with diversified funding streams, performance-based milestones, and shared infra costs for emulation environments.
Create a sovereign fund-backed identity resilience incubator that licenses open components to government, monetizes certifications, and shares revenue from resilience services.
review: Trade-off: Controls Cost vs. Sovereign Autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider sector-specific budget volatility and political turnover.

name: Public Trust & Privacy Governance
consequences: Immediate: Public scrutiny increases legitimacy → Systemic: 25% faster alignment to privacy norms via shared dashboards → Strategic: builds trust, but may slow decision cycles and invite activist pressure.
options: Publish transparent risk assessments and establish an oversight panel with civil-society participation and public reporting.
Pilot participatory design workshops, publish privacy-by-design toolkits, and implement routine privacy impact assessments across procurement and standards activities.
Adopt an open-source identity stack governed by community consensus with continuous privacy-impact auditing marketplace and public accountability dashboards.
review: Trade-off: Controls Transparency vs. Decision Agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential information overload and security incident response delays.

name: Platform-Neutral Identity Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Dependency on a single platform trust anchor is reduced; teams test platform-neutral components. Systemic: 25% faster scaling through parallel verifiers and cross-agency credential pools. Strategic: Sovereignty gains but governance overhead increases.
options: Adopt a platform-neutral identity framework anchored in FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect, and NSIS components, deployed via open government verifiers with a shared policy model.
Develop interoperable fallback connectors using portable auth agents and hardware tokens that can operate without Apple/Google signals, with phased integration into AltID procurement language.

Deploy a decentralized identity fabric using Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) with verifiable credentials and DIDs, supported by edge-cloud verifiers and an open-source governance foundation to enable platform-neutral access.
review: Trade-off explicitly: 'Controls platform lock-in vs. governance overhead'. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory and procurement cost implications of SSI deployment and cross-EU interoperability.

name: Procurement & Standards Leverage
consequences: Immediate: Procurement language shifts toward resilience and neutrality; agencies begin pilots with fallback-ready vendors. Systemic: 25% faster scaling through shared certification standards and cross-department reuse of evaluation data. Strategic: Bargaining power increases, yet vendor coalitions may slow rapid local innovation.
options: Codify platform-neutrality in AltID procurement documents, require fallback capabilities, and align with NSIS/eIDAS; pilot with limited departments.
Develop a shared security benchmarking framework focusing on resilience, portability, and auditability; require annual independent verification and publish results.
Establish a public-private platform-neutral consortium to fund an open-source ecosystem of identity providers; introduce a pay-for-use model and mandated interoperability in procurement.
review: Trade-off explicitly: 'Controls vendor lock-in vs. governance overhead'. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost and timeline variability of EU-wide interoperability requirements and small-vendor capacity.

name: Coalition-Building & Narrative Framing
consequences: Immediate: Public engagement efforts accelerate coalition formation and legitimacy. Systemic: 25% faster scaling through shared public metrics and cross-agency collaboration. Strategic: Greater democratic legitimacy, but potential political antagonism and conflicting interests.
options: Institutionalize resilience framing within existing governance, establishing a formal fallback plan with defined SLAs and privacy accountability.
Create a cross-sector coalition to sponsor platform-neutral pilot with transparent metrics and independent security verification.
Establish Identity Commons with token-based governance, SSI, verifiable credentials, and community treasury funding platform-neutral providers.
review: Trade-off explicitly: 'Controls public legitimacy vs. speed of policy adoption'. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of politicization and misinformation campaigns affecting procurement.

name: Technical Demonstrators & Standards Demonstration
consequences: Immediate: Demonstrators validate viability with non-production scope. Systemic: 25% faster scaling through shared test results and cross-agency adoption. Strategic: Demonstrations reduce procurement risk but may reveal certification gaps.
options: Prototype a minimal viable demonstration on GrapheneOS using FIDO2/WebAuthn with a hardware-token fallback, keeping scope limited and non-production.
Build a standards-based reference implementation interoperable with EU wallet; test in a closed lab and gather metrics for fallback viability.
Develop an open-edge identity fabric leveraging zero-knowledge proofs, secure enclaves, and an experimental marketplace for identity services.
review: Trade-off explicitly: 'Controls demonstration scope vs. certification readiness'. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term integration with MitID and EU wallet pipelines.

name: Legal/Regulatory & Procurement Readiness
consequences: Immediate: Legal alignment advances; agencies revise procurement rules and RFP templates. Systemic: 25% faster scaling through cross-border interoperability guidance and shared regulatory playbooks. Strategic: Enhanced sovereignty, but increased regulatory friction and longer lead times.
options: Align Danish law with eIDAS and NSIS; draft fallback policy documents; propose platform-neutral procurement rules.
Create a regulatory sandbox and EU wallet interoperability task force; run a department-level pilot.
Pursue legal reforms that designate platform-neutrality as critical infrastructure and establish a sovereign procurement fund with cross-border agreements.
review: Trade-off explicitly: 'Controls rapid policy alignment vs. regulatory capture'. Weakness: The options fail to consider budget constraints and cross-department coordination challenges.
